data.title: Blockchain Just Had Its First Bank Run: Key Investor Takeaways
data.description:
After the $294 million hack at Kelp DAO last weekend, traders yanked over $15 billion from major protocols. It’s as close as DeFi's ever come to an old-school bank run, sending shockwaves across DeFi markets. The twist? You didn't have to use Kelp to feel it.

Welcome to DeFi's new reality. If you make a bet on one protocol, you need to reckon with every other chain it touches.

The Hidden Risk In Your Yield

DeFi's core appeal hasn't changed, but restaking protocols like Kelp DAO have broadened it. Instead of earning from just one source of yield, you can now stack multiple types of return atop the same underlying asset. Deposit ETH, receive a liquid token like rsETH, then deploy that token across lending markets, liquidity pools, or other strategies.

It sounds like a winner: Capital stays productive and your earning potential rises. But so does complexity.

That rsETH token was plugged into multiple major protocols as collateral. When 116,500 rsETH, roughly 18% of its circulating supply, was drained in the exploit, the problem mushroomed. Platforms that had integrated rsETH had to react quickly.

Some paused markets. Others saw users rush to withdraw funds or unwind positions, thinning out liquidity. If you were holding or using rsETH anywhere in the stack, directly or indirectly, you were exposed.

Connectivity Cuts Both Ways

One of DeFi's biggest strengths is its interoperability. Developers can stack protocols like financial Lego, allowing crypto investors to do very cool things: like borrow against a liquid staking token and then reinvest the borrowed funds to get more yield.

It's efficient and flexible. In the rush of a bull market it can feel almost riskless. But it also creates shared points of failure.

The Kelp DAO exploit looks to have originated in a cross-chain bridge setup. Attackers sent a forged message that tricked the system into believing funds had arrived from another blockchain. The protocol released tokens it shouldn't have.

One critical validation relied on a single approval mechanism. Once that weak link broke, a flood of troubles headed downstream. The incident has exposed an awkward compromise. The more integrated and flexible decentralized finance becomes, the more any single problem can propagate.

A Wider Attack Surface

Blockchain security firms have been warning for months that crypto attacks are getting more sophisticated and targeting higher-value operations. Groups linked to North Korea are developing coordinated tactical campaigns that can take months to execute.

The Kelp exploit fits that pattern, as did the recent $285 million attack on Solana-based platform Drift. Attackers are increasingly using AI to scan smart contract code, identify edge-case vulnerabilities, and simulate attack paths at scale. Hacks that once took take weeks of trial and error can now be AI-automated, and execute in a matter of hours.

Of course traditional finance gets hacked too, but there are built-in recovery options. Transactions can be flagged, paused, or even reversed. In DeFi, code is law. If a transaction executes successfully on-chain, it's usually final. Funds can be moved, mixed, and bridged across ecosystems before anyone has time to react. 

By the time a protocol pauses contracts or freezes activity, the damage is probably done. In the Kelp DAO case, emergency controls reportedly stopped an additional $100 million from being drained. But the bulk of the funds were already gone.

Where DeFi Goes From Here

Is DeFi broken? Not completely, but the narrative of easy gains is over. The next phase of growth will depend on resilience. In practice that will mean:

  • More robust validator setups
  • Better real-time monitoring
  • Faster response mechanisms when things go wrong
  • And probably, slower rollout of complex features

For crypto investors, it will also mean recalibrating expectations. Opportunities for bigger gains will still be there, but tied to increasingly complex and interconnected systems. Higher yield. Higher blast radius.

Takeaways For Investors

1. Yield as a risk signal
Any strategy that offers meaningflully higher returns probably comes with additional layers of risk. That’s not new, but in DeFi it plays out through smart contract risk, integration risk, and liquidity risk – potentially all at once.

2. Your exposure isn't always obvious
You might not be using a protocol directly, but if your collateral, LP tokens, or yield strategies might depend on it.

3. Complexity compounds risk
Stacking protocols can boost returns. It also increases the number of ways a position can break.

4. Liquidity can disappear fast
When confidence drops, withdrawals accelerate. That can amplify losses, especially in leveraged or collateralized positions.

Image credit: Author

Benzinga Disclaimer: This article is from an unpaid external contributor. It does not represent Benzinga’s reporting and has not been edited for content or accuracy.

data.title: What's Going On With TotalEnergies Stock Friday?
data.description:
The project combines 1 GW of onshore wind capacity with a 600 MWh battery system to improve grid reliability. It is expected to generate 100 TWh of electricity over 25 years—enough to power roughly 1 million people—under a long-term power purchase agreement with the government.

TotalEnergies holds a 60% stake, alongside Samruk Energy and KazMunayGas.

Technical Analysis

TotalEnergies is still trading near the top of its 52-week range, sitting just under the April peak near $93.49, which keeps the bigger-picture uptrend in focus. The stock is trading 0.7% below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 17.7% above its 100-day SMA, a mix that points to short-term cooling but a still-firm intermediate trend.

The moving average convergence divergence (MACD), a trend/momentum measure, is below its signal line and the histogram is negative, which leans toward fading upside momentum after the April run. When MACD sits under its signal, it often reflects buyers losing control of the near-term pace even if the trend is still up.

  • Key Resistance: $93.50 — where recent rallies have stalled near the 52-week high area.
  • Key Support: $85.50 — a level that’s acted like a floor during pullbacks.

Earnings & Analyst Outlook

The countdown is on: TotalEnergies SE is set to report earnings on April 29, 2026 (confirmed).

  • EPS Estimate: $1.96 (Up from $1.83 YoY)
  • Revenue Estimate: $46.36 Billion (Down from $52.25 Billion YoY)
  • Valuation: P/E of 15.7x (Suggests fair valuation relative to peers)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Hold rating with an average price target of $83.00. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Scotiabank: Sector Perform (Raises Target to $97.00) (April 22)
  • Piper Sandler: Neutral (Raises Target to $92.00) (March 12)
  • JP Morgan: Upgraded to Overweight (March 2)

Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for TotalEnergies, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Momentum: Bullish (Score: 88.24) — The stock is showing strong trend persistence versus the broader market.
  • Value: Bullish (Score: 88.8) — The setup screens as attractively priced versus many peers on value factors.
  • Growth: Neutral (Score: 51.39) — Growth characteristics look middle-of-the-pack rather than a clear tailwind.

The Verdict: TotalEnergies’ Benzinga Edge signal reveals a momentum-plus-value profile, where trend strength is doing a lot of the heavy lifting. With growth scoring closer to neutral, the next test is whether the stock can hold support near $85.50 if the broader market keeps favoring tech-led risk-on trades.

Top ETF Exposure

Price Action

TTE Stock Price Activity: TotalEnergies shares were down 0.80% at $89.75 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Shares are declining as energy names lag a risk-on tape that’s rotating hard into growth and tech. The Nasdaq is up 1.76% while the S&P 500 has gained 0.76%.

Photo by Vytautas Kielaitis via Shutterstock

data.title: Intel Reports Q1 Beat, Yet These Analysts Remain Bearish
data.description:
Here are the key analyst insights:

Check out other analyst stock ratings.

BofA Securities: Intel reported strong quarter results and announced a healthy outlook, due to "accelerating demand and pricing power in server CPU driving agentic AI workloads," Arya said in a note. He added, however, that Intel's gross margins remained "subpar," with the company continuing to burn cash.

Despite the meaningful earnings beat, the analyst maintained an Underperform rating, citing:

  • Intel's stock already pricing in the company's recovery
  • Management projecting sales CAGR (compounded annual growth rate) of 10%-15% for 2025-2028, well below peers at 30%-40%
  • Intel's foundry "still needs to prove for external customers"

JPMorgan: Intel's first-quarter results and guidance for the second quarter came in higher than expectations across the board, Sur said. He added, however, that the quality of earnings remains an issue.

The analyst cited the following reasons for maintaining the Underweight rating:

Rosenblatt Securities: Intel reported revenues of $13.58 billion and non-GAAP earnings of 29 cents per share, topping consensus estimates of $12.42 billion and 2 cents per share, respectively, Cassidy said. Non-GAAP gross margins contracted by 0.7% sequentially to 41.0%, he added.

Intel guided to second-quarter revenue of $14.3 billion at the midpoint, above consensus of $13.1 billion, the analyst stated. "Management now expects the PC unit TAM to decline in the low double digits while server CPU units grow at a double-digit rate, with this momentum extending into CY2027," he further wrote.

Needham: Intel indicated that its revenue in the first quarter could have been higher by more than $1 billion if supply was not this tight, Bolton said. Strength in server CPU is expected to continue due to demand from AI infrastructure, with the company projecting double-digit growth in industry units in 2026 and the momentum continuing in 2027, he added.

"The company’s foundry revenue came in above expectations as management noted that 18A yields are ahead of internal projections," the analyst wrote. The company expects 2026 capex to be higher than it had earlier projected, he further stated.

INTC Price Action: Shares of Intel had risen by 22.87% to $82.05 at the time of publication on Friday.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Meta Goes All-In On Amazon's Beast Chips
data.description:
Amazon And Meta Expand AWS Graviton Partnership

Meta has penned an agreement to utilize AWS Graviton processors, marking a significant expansion of their partnership.

This deployment will begin with tens of millions of Graviton cores, allowing Meta to enhance its AI capabilities, which require substantial processing power.

With this deal, Meta is now one of the largest Graviton customers in the world.

The chips will power various workloads at Meta, including supporting the company’s AI efforts.

That work requires infrastructure capable of handling billions of interactions while coordinating complex, multi-step agent workflows.

Amazon Technical Analysis

Amazon is trading near its 52-week high, reflecting strong bullish momentum. The stock is currently 12.3% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 18.9% above its 50-day SMA, indicating a robust short-term trend.

The relative strength index (RSI) is at 77.33, suggesting that the stock is in overbought territory, which may indicate a potential pullback. This high RSI level typically reflects strong buying pressure, but traders should be cautious as it could signal a reversal.

  • Key Resistance: $258.50 — A level where selling pressure may emerge.
  • Key Support: $226.50 — A level that could provide buying interest if tested.

Amazon has shown a strong 12-month performance of 38.59%, indicating a positive long-term trend. This performance suggests that the stock has been on a solid upward trajectory, attracting investor interest.

Earnings Preview And Analyst Price Targets

The countdown is on: Amazon.com is set to report earnings on April 29, 2026 (confirmed).

  • EPS Estimate: $1.64 (Up from $1.59)
  • Revenue Estimate: $177.31 Billion (Up from $155.70 Billion)
  • Valuation: P/E of 35.6x (Indicates premium valuation)

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $290.81. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Oppenheimer: Outperform (Raises Target to $275.00) (April 24)
  • UBS: Buy (Raises Target to $304.00) (April 23)
  • BMO Capital: Outperform (Raises Target to $315.00) (April 23)

Top ETFs With Significant AMZN Exposure

Significance: Because AMZN carries such a heavy weight in these funds, any significant inflows or outflows for these ETFs will likely force automatic buying or selling of the stock.

AMZN Price Action: Amazon.com shares were up 2.74% at $262.08 at the time of publication on Friday. The stock is trading at a new 52-week high, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: SpaceX Is 30% Overvalued, 'Dean Of Valuation' Says: Why He's Not Buying The Moonshot Numbers
data.description:
NYU Stern professor Aswath Damodaran published a valuation of SpaceX on Thursday at $1.2 trillion, roughly a third below the $1.75 trillion to $2 trillion range the company is reportedly targeting for its IPO.

The “Dean of Valuation” built the number from scratch without a prospectus, breaking the company into three businesses: space launch, Starlink, and xAI, which SpaceX absorbed in February, plus an “expansion option” for adjacent markets like space travel.

His model has revenues climbing from $15.6 billion in 2025 to roughly $320 billion a decade out, with a consolidated operating margin near 48%. Starlink does most of the work. It made up around 65% of 2025 revenues. The rockets were 30%.

“This is a big story with a big payoff,” Damodaran said. His $1.2 trillion base case landed “about 10% below the private market pricing” and “a third below what people are expecting the pricing to be.”

The Polymarket Scorecard

Traders are not with him, but they may not be measuring the same thing. As Damodaran himself put it, “companies are priced, not valued” at IPO. A hype-driven listing can clear well above fair value and Damodaran can still be right on the fundamentals.

Polymarket’s SpaceX valuation contract prices the $1.75 trillion to $2 trillion bracket at 48%. Damodaran’s number falls into the $1.25 trillion to $1.50 trillion bucket, which traders are pricing at 11%.

A separate contract gives SpaceX a 90% probability of being the largest 2026 IPO by market cap, and traders expect the IPO to be completed by the end of June.

The Musk Package

Damodaran has owned Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) since 2019 and called SpaceX Elon Musk’s “most impressive contribution.” He also warned investors not to cherry-pick.

“You get the package. You get the Musk package, the good stuff, the bad stuff,” he said. “It’s not fair complaining about the things about Musk you don’t like because they come bundled in.”

To account for the uncertainty, Damodaran ran a Monte Carlo simulation that spat out a range from $660 billion to $2.8 trillion, with a median of $1.28 trillion. A $1.75 trillion IPO would land at the upper end of that distribution.

He said he would be “reluctant to buy SpaceX” at that price. Closer to $1 trillion, he would be a buyer.

Image: Shutterstock
